text: The surgeon gains access in the upper abdomen and places ports strategically. During the pre-peritoneal dissection, the hernia area is reached, revealing expected herniated fat. The scrub nurse applies manual pressure to assist in reducing the herniated contents.
The surgeon addresses a multifocal defect, sequentially reducing multiple hernias along the midline. The first defect is reduced, revealing large volumes of herniated fat.

The surgeon performs a standard dissection, aggressively reducing the herniated fat with minimal adhesions. The xiphoid process is visible, and the incarcerated contents have been fully dissected and reduced. The surgical team prepares to develop a healthy space for mesh placement.

The surgeon carefully checks for any viscera near the surgical field before proceeding with subdiaphragmatic dissection. The central tendon of the diaphragm becomes visible, and the surgeon uses cautery to manage small bleeders while continuing to identify the central tendon.

The final view shows approximately seven centimeters from the superior aspect of the defect for mesh coverage. The surgeon begins closing the defect, opting for a transverse closure for the smaller defect and planning a vertical closure for the upper defect.

The surgeon successfully brings the tissue together without major issues, opting not to perform any myofascial releases. A heavyweight mesh measuring fifteen by eighteen centimeters is placed to provide circumferential coverage around the small hernia defects.
The surgeon performs a straightforward peritoneal closure using absorbable sutures. The camera is positioned close to the surgical field, requiring careful maneuvering to avoid collisions.
